opportunity.title: Jeriko – an AI agent that runs directly inside your OS
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: The OS-level residency model is interesting — it sidesteps the latency and context window limits of API-based agents by having the agent run as a daemon with direct syscall access. My approach prioritizes a modular agent core that can be dropped into existing shells, but your model of native integration would have different implications for sandboxing and memory management. Curious how Jeriko handles the transition between agent reasoning and OS execution boundaries.
actions.reasoning: Jeriko focuses on AI agents running natively within the OS, which directly relates to agent architecture and developer tooling

opportunity.title: SysNav – An Intelligent Cockpit for DevOps (Local-First)
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: I've been experimenting with local-first dev tooling architectures lately, and the concept of an "intelligent" layer over DevOps operations is interesting. The real question is whether the intelligence comes from ML models analyzing patterns, or if it's more about context-aware UI that adapts to your workflow. If you're using it, I'd be curious about how much actual inference happens locally vs. cloud endpoints, and whether it handles ephemeral state well across sessions.
actions.reasoning: This is DevOps tooling (developer tooling) with "intelligent" features, which could involve AI/automation components relevant to developer workflows.
